title: Test GPT-5.3-codex with reasoningEffort:low on mechanical task
description:
## Problem

GPT-5.3-codex used 150K tokens (55 reasoning parts) vs Claude's 36K tokens (2 reasoning parts) on comparable code extraction tasks. The reasoningEffort is set to 'medium' by default. For mechanical tasks like code extraction, lower reasoning effort may produce identical quality at fraction of token cost.

## Experiment

1. Pick another mechanical task (e.g. orch-go-utvzp complete_cmd extraction if not done, or similar)
2. Spawn with --model openai/gpt-5.3-codex and reasoningEffort:low variant
3. Compare: token count, reasoning parts, output quality, completion time
4. Baseline: og-feat-extract-doctor-go-06feb-a099 (medium effort, 150K tokens, 15 min)

## Expected outcome

If low reasoning produces same quality: GPT becomes viable for routine work
If quality degrades: reasoning effort is load-bearing for GPT agents

## Config reference

~/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc - gpt-5.3-codex model variants

title: Side-by-side code quality review: GPT-5.3-codex vs Claude Opus extraction
description:
## Problem

We compared GPT-5.3-codex and Claude Opus on code extraction tasks by metrics (tokens, time, messages) but never compared the actual output quality. Need to evaluate the code itself.

## Approach

Compare these two completed extractions:
- GPT-5.3: og-feat-extract-doctor-go-06feb-a099 (doctor.go → 5 modules)
- Claude: og-feat-extract-status-cmd-06feb-54b5 (status_cmd.go → 4 modules)

Evaluate:
- File structure and naming conventions
- Function organization and cohesion
- Idiomatic Go patterns
- Test coverage and quality
- Error handling
- Comments and documentation
- Whether files stay under 500 line target

## Deliverable

Investigation with head-to-head comparison and recommendation for which model produces cleaner extractions.

title: Test GPT-5.3-codex on non-mechanical task (debugging or investigation)
description:
## Problem

Code extraction is the friendliest possible test for multi-model support — clear input, clear output, no ambiguity. We need to know where GPT-5.3 breaks or excels on tasks requiring judgment: systematic-debugging (hypothesis-testing), investigation (synthesis writing), or architect (trade-off analysis).

## Experiment

1. Pick a real debugging or investigation issue from backlog
2. Spawn with --model openai/gpt-5.3-codex
3. Evaluate: Does it follow the skill protocol? Does it produce useful artifacts? Does it get stuck?
4. Compare reasoning part count and token usage for judgment-heavy work

## Key question

GPT produced 2 text parts vs Claude's 28 on extraction (GPT acts silently, Claude narrates). For investigation/architect skills that require written synthesis, does GPT's silent style produce worse artifacts?

## Candidates

- Any open P2 bug or investigation task
- Ideally something where we have a Claude baseline to compare

title: Research ChatGPT Pro subscription quota structure for GPT-5.3
description:
## Problem

GPT-5.3-codex consumed 150K tokens on a single code extraction task. If ChatGPT Pro has a usage cap that counts reasoning tokens, this dramatically limits the number of tasks per billing period. We need to understand the actual economics before committing to multi-model routing.

## Questions

1. What is the Pro subscription rate limit? (tokens/day, tokens/month, requests/hour?)
2. Do reasoning tokens count against the limit?
3. What happens when you hit the limit? (throttled, blocked, degraded?)
4. Is there a difference between gpt-5.3-codex and gpt-5.2 quotas?
5. How does this compare to Claude Max subscription limits?

## Approach

Research via OpenAI docs, community reports, and empirical testing (check headers for rate limit info after API calls).

comments.text: Phase: Planning - Reading prior research and scoping ChatGPT Pro quota investigation for GPT-5.3

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Scope: 1. Research ChatGPT Pro rate limits for GPT-5.3 (tokens/day, month, req/hour) 2. Determine if reasoning tokens count against limit 3. What happens at limit (throttle/block/degrade) 4. Compare gpt-5.3-codex vs gpt-5.2 quotas 5. Compare to Claude Max limits 6. Write investigation file with findings

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Implementing - Writing investigation file. Key findings: GPT-5.3-Codex exists (Codex-only, no API yet), ChatGPT Pro claims unlimited but has message-based limits (300-1500 local/5h), reasoning tokens ARE billed as output, credits system at ~5 credits/local msg

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Research investigation written. Key findings: (1) ChatGPT Pro limits are MESSAGE-based not token-based (300-1500 local msgs/5h), making 150K token tasks less concerning. (2) GPT-5.3-Codex exists but is Codex-only (no API yet). (3) Reasoning tokens billed as output on API but absorbed into message limits on subscription. (4) All Codex models share same quotas. (5) For high-token work, ChatGPT Pro message-based limits may be more generous than Claude Max credit-based limits. Tests: N/A (research task). Leave it Better: kb-53a46f (decision), kb-0065b0 (constraint). No discovered work.

title: Investigate token counting discrepancy between GPT and Claude in OpenCode
description:
## Problem

GPT-5.3-codex reported 150K tokens but only 10.3MB on disk. Claude Opus reported 36K tokens but 17.5MB on disk. The token counts may not be apples-to-apples — Claude may exclude thinking tokens that GPT includes, or the counting methodology differs per provider.

## Evidence

Session storage comparison:
- GPT: 57 messages, 228 parts, 10.3MB disk, 150K reported tokens
- Claude: 39 messages, 156 parts, 17.5MB disk, 36K reported tokens

Part type breakdown:
- GPT: 55 reasoning, 59 tool, 2 text
- Claude: 2 reasoning, 50 tool, 28 text

## Questions

1. Does OpenCode count reasoning tokens in the reported total for OpenAI but not Anthropic?
2. Are Claude's extended thinking tokens (budgetTokens: 32000) excluded from the count?
3. What exactly does the token count in orch status represent per provider?

## Approach

Check OpenCode source for token counting logic per provider. Check API response headers for actual usage data.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Investigating token counting discrepancy between GPT and Claude in OpenCode. Will examine source code for per-provider token counting logic.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Investigation complete. Token counts ARE apples-to-apples: OpenAI reports reasoning tokens separately (tokens.reasoning), Anthropic includes thinking in output_tokens (reasoning always 0). Both totals represent full usage. Also found multi-step token overwrite bug in processor.ts:244 (tokens overwritten not accumulated per step). Investigation file: .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-token-counting-discrepancy-gpt-vs-claude.md. Tests: N/A - investigation only, code read verified against sources. Leave it Better: kb quick decide recorded. No discovered work.

title: Design multi-model routing strategy for orch spawn
description:
## Problem

With GPT-5.3-codex proven functional in our spawn pipeline, we need a routing strategy: which tasks go to which model, and how does the orchestrator/daemon decide?

## Depends on

Results from:
- Reasoning effort experiment (does low work for mechanical tasks?)
- Code quality comparison (is GPT output comparable?)
- Non-mechanical task test (where does GPT struggle?)
- Quota research (what are the economics?)
- Token counting investigation (are we measuring correctly?)

## Design questions

1. Should routing be by skill type? (extraction→GPT, debugging→Claude)
2. Should routing be by quota? (overflow to GPT when Claude is high)
3. Should routing be configurable per-project?
4. How does the daemon decide which model to use?
5. Should the orchestrator have a --model preference in orch focus?

## Deliverable

Decision record with routing rules and daemon integration plan.

title: Completion gate ceremony is the primary orchestrator bottleneck
description:
## Problem

The orch complete verification gate system was designed for careful single-agent review but is now the primary friction point for multi-agent parallel workflows. Every completion requires discovering which gates fail, then adding the right combination of --skip-X --skip-reason flags.

## Evidence from live session

- 26 completions took 5+ minutes with two 120s timeouts
- Common pattern: --skip-build (broken by other agents) + --skip-dashboard-health (Go refactor not dashboard) + --skip-phase-complete (dead session) + --skip-synthesis (light tier)
- Each --skip-reason requires min 10 chars, adding friction to every invocation
- Error messages don't list WHICH gate failed - just 'verification failed' with a wall of available skip flags

## Solution

1. **Show which gate failed** - 'verification failed: BUILD_GATE (go build exit 1)' instead of generic error
2. **Reduce skip ceremony** - single --skip-all-reason 'batch close after review' instead of per-gate reasons
3. **Smart defaults** - if build is broken system-wide, don't gate every completion on it
4. **Batch mode** - orch review done --reviewed 'reason' skips all gates (see orch-go-21345)

## Files likely affected
- cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go (gate pipeline, error reporting)
- cmd/orch/review.go (batch completion path)

title: orch complete error messages don't identify which gate failed
description:
## Problem

When orch complete fails verification, it prints 'verification failed' followed by the full list of available --skip flags. It does NOT tell you which gate failed or what the failure was. This forces trial-and-error: add --skip-build, try again, still fails, add --skip-dashboard-health, try again, etc.

## Solution

Print the specific gate failure:
```
Verification failed:
  ✗ BUILD_GATE: go build exit code 1 (cmd/orch/complete_helpers.go:21:6 redeclared)
  ✗ DASHBOARD_HEALTH: Changes to serve_*.go require dashboard verification
  ✓ PHASE_COMPLETE: Phase: Complete comment found
  ✓ SKILL_OUTPUT: Investigation artifact exists

Skip failing gates with:
  --skip-build --skip-dashboard-health --skip-reason 'reason'
```

## Files affected
- cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go (verification pipeline, error formatting)

comments.author: dylanconlin

When verification fails, now shows each gate with colored checkmark/cross and error detail, plus exact --skip flags needed. Tests: go test ./pkg/verify/... - ok (6.880s), go build ./... - passed. No discovered work.

title: Dead sessions have no retry limit or escalation - zombie respawn loop
description:
## Problem

Three issues (orch-go-21294, 21315, 21275) died twice each without completing. Each time the dead session detector reset them to open, and the daemon respawned them. There is no retry limit, no escalation after repeated failures, and no mechanism to detect 'this keeps dying.'

## Evidence

- All three had root cause identified in Phase: Implementing comments
- All three died mid-implementation (likely context exhaustion or server restart)  
- On respawn, new agents don't read prior beads comments, so partial work is lost
- No label or metadata tracks retry count

## Solution

1. **Track retry count** - bd metadata or label like 'retry:2' incremented on each dead session
2. **Escalation threshold** - After N failures (default 2), label as 'needs:human' and stop daemon respawning
3. **Preserve context across retries** - Include prior agent's beads comments in respawn context so new agent sees partial findings
4. **Dead session summary** - When detecting dead session, extract last Phase comment and include in issue description

## Files likely affected
- pkg/daemon/daemon.go (retry tracking, escalation logic)
- cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go (dead session detection)
- pkg/spawn/context.go (include prior attempt context)

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Investigating dead session retry loop. Reading daemon.go, complete_cmd.go, and context.go to understand current dead session detection and respawn flow.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Implementing - Root cause identified: MarkSessionAsDead unconditionally resets to open with no retry tracking. Fix: count DEAD SESSION comments on issue, escalate to needs:human after threshold (default 2), extract last Phase comment for context.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
DEAD SESSION: Session died without completing (no active session, no Phase: Complete)

The spawned agent session died without completing. This can happen due to:
- Agent crash or context exhaustion
- OpenCode server restart
- Manual session termination

Status reset to 'open' for respawning.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Root cause: MarkSessionAsDead unconditionally resets to open with no retry tracking, enabling infinite zombie respawn loops. Fix: (1) Count DEAD SESSION comments on issue as retry count source of truth, (2) Escalate to needs:human after threshold (default 2) with EscalateDeadSession, (3) Preserve last Phase comment in dead session comments for context. Tests: go test ./pkg/daemon/ -run TestDeadSession - 4 tests, all passed. Also includes syntax fix for corrupted grace_period code in daemon.go. No discovered work.

title: Daemon grabs triage:ready issues before orchestrator can adjust - needs staging mechanism
description:
## Problem

When labeling issues triage:ready, the daemon polls and grabs them within seconds. If the orchestrator needs to adjust (wrong model, needs manual spawn context, label mistake), the daemon has already spawned. Must abandon + respawn, wasting a session.

## Evidence

Labeled 2 GPT-specific issues triage:ready, immediately realized they needed --model openai/gpt-5.3-codex. Removed labels within seconds but daemon already grabbed both. Had to abandon both agents and respawn manually.

## Solution Options

1. **Staging label** - 'triage:staging' that allows review before 'triage:ready' (two-step release)
2. **Daemon grace period** - Don't spawn issues for N seconds after triage:ready label added (allows corrections)
3. **bd create --hold** - Create with triage:ready but add a hold that daemon respects until released
4. **Model hint label** - 'model:gpt-5.3-codex' label that daemon reads when spawning

Option 4 is interesting because it solves both problems: daemon respects model preference AND orchestrator doesn't need manual spawn for model selection.

## Files likely affected
- pkg/daemon/daemon.go (polling/grace period or model label support)  
- cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go (model label reading)

title: Orchestrator skill needs 'messy backlog' playbook for inherited completion debt
description:
## Problem

The orchestrator skill describes a clean pipeline: agent completes → orch review → synthesize → close. In practice, a new orchestrator session inherits a messy backlog: mixed tracked/untracked agents from different eras, escalations buried in rubber stamps, dead sessions, and different commands needed for different agent types.

## Evidence

This session inherited:
- 29 unverified completions (22 rubber-stamp, 3 escalations, 1 spawn, 2 partial)
- 26 old untracked agents needing individual orch complete with skip flags
- 3 dead sessions that had respawned and died again
- All looking identical in dashboard ('NEEDS REVIEW' on everything)

## Skill gaps

1. No triage protocol for inherited backlog ('start session with orch review, handle in order: escalations → partials → rubber stamps')
2. No guidance on untracked vs tracked agent differences
3. No playbook for dead session zombies
4. No concept of 'completion debt' as a thing to manage

## Solution

Add 'Backlog Triage' section to orchestrator skill covering:
- Session start: run orch review, categorize by rec type
- Handle escalations first (need decisions)
- Bulk close rubber stamps (orch review done --reviewed)
- Dead sessions: check retry count, escalate or close with reason
- Untracked agents: clean with orch clean --stale or individual complete

## Files affected
- Orchestrator skill templates (~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/meta/orchestrator/)

title: System-wide build failure should suppress build gate on all completions
description:
## Problem

When concurrent extraction agents break the build (duplicate declarations), EVERY subsequent orch complete fails the build gate. The orchestrator must add --skip-build --skip-reason on every single completion even though the build failure is system-wide and unrelated to the specific agent being completed.

## Evidence

4 extraction agents ran concurrently. One created complete_actions.go with functions still in complete_cmd.go → build broken. Every completion after that required --skip-build, including agents that didn't touch those files.

## Solution

1. **Detect system-wide build failure** - If build has been failing for N minutes, auto-skip build gate with warning
2. **Blame attribution** - Check if the build failure is caused by THIS agent's commits vs pre-existing. Only gate if this agent broke it.
3. **Build gate memory** - Once orchestrator skips build gate with a reason, remember that reason for subsequent completions in same session

## Files affected
- cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go (build gate logic, blame attribution)

title: Architect: Redesign completion pipeline for multi-agent parallel workflows
description:
## Problem

The completion/verification pipeline was designed for single-agent sequential review. It's now the primary orchestrator bottleneck in multi-agent parallel workflows. Three related issues stem from the same root cause:

- **orch-go-21351**: Gate ceremony requires 3-5 skip flags per completion, each with reason strings. 26 agents took 5+ minutes.
- **orch-go-21345**: No bulk close path for already-reviewed completions.  
- **orch-go-21356**: System-wide build failures gate every completion even when unrelated to the agent.

## Design Questions

1. **Which gates are load-bearing vs theater?** Phase: Complete, build, test evidence, synthesis — which actually catch problems vs add friction?
2. **What's the trust model?** Should there be 'careful mode' (current) vs 'batch mode' (post-review rubber stamp)?
3. **How should skip reasons work?** Per-gate reasons add friction. Session-level reasons ('build broken by other agents') could apply to all completions.
4. **Blame attribution**: Should build gate check if THIS agent broke the build vs pre-existing failure?
5. **Gate memory**: If orchestrator skips a gate with reason, should subsequent completions in the session remember?

## Evidence

From live triage session (Feb 6, 2026):
- 26 untracked agents: each needed individual orch complete with 3-5 skip flags
- 5 daemon agents: 2 died without Phase: Complete, 3 completed but build gate failed (broken by concurrent extractions)
- Total completion time: 5+ minutes with two 120s timeouts
- Orchestrator spent more time fighting gates than reviewing actual work

## Constraints

- Gates must remain available for high-risk work (new features, complex debugging)
- Bulk mode must still produce audit trail (who reviewed, when, why)
- Must work for both tracked (beads) and untracked (legacy) agents

## Deliverable

Decision record with: gate tier model, batch completion design, skip reason simplification, blame attribution approach.

title: Architect: Daemon resilience - retry limits, escalation, and staging
description:
## Problem

Two daemon behaviors create friction in multi-agent workflows:

**Zombie respawn loop (orch-go-21353):** Dead sessions get reset to open, daemon respawns, agent dies again. No retry limit, no escalation, no context preservation. Three issues died twice each this session.

**Race condition staging (orch-go-21354):** Daemon grabs triage:ready issues within seconds of labeling. If orchestrator needs to adjust (model selection, spawn context), must abandon + respawn. No staging mechanism.

## Design Questions

### Retry & Escalation
1. Where to store retry count? (beads metadata, label like retry:2, issue field?)
2. What's the escalation threshold? (2 failures? 3?)
3. What label/status for escalated issues? (needs:human? triage:review?)
4. How to preserve prior attempt context? (Include last Phase comment in respawn context? Link to archived workspace?)
5. Should daemon track failure reasons to detect patterns? (all dying at same phase → systemic issue)

### Staging & Model Routing  
6. Best staging mechanism? Options:
   a. Two-step label: triage:staging → triage:ready
   b. Grace period: don't spawn for N seconds after label
   c. Hold flag: bd create --hold
   d. Model hint label: model:gpt-5.3-codex (daemon reads on spawn)
7. Option D solves both staging AND multi-model routing — daemon reads model label and spawns accordingly. Worth the complexity?
8. How does this interact with the GPT integration work (orch-go-21350)?

## Evidence

From live session:
- 3 dead sessions: orch-go-21294, 21315, 21275 — root causes found in Phase: Implementing but never landed
- 2 GPT-specific issues grabbed by daemon before model could be specified — required abandon + respawn
- No retry count tracking anywhere in the system

## Deliverable

Decision record with: retry tracking design, escalation protocol, staging mechanism choice, model routing integration.

title: Experiment: GPT-5.3-codex with model-agnostic prompt vs Claude-tuned prompt
description:
## Problem

GPT-5.3-codex scored 2.9/5 on code extraction (non-compiling output, duplicate functions left in source). But our entire spawn system — SPAWN_CONTEXT.md format, skill templates, phase protocol, extraction guides — was designed for Claude's working style. We can't distinguish 'GPT is worse at code' from 'our prompts are Claude-optimized' without a controlled experiment.

## Key observations suggesting prompt bias

1. **Claude narrates (28 text parts), GPT reasons silently (2 text parts)** — our skills assume a model that explains its plan before acting. GPT's silent reasoning may process implicit instructions differently.
2. **Extraction guide assumes Claude's approach** — .kb/guides/code-extraction-patterns.md was written for Claude. The critical step 'remove extracted functions from the original file' is implicit, not explicit.
3. **SPAWN_CONTEXT.md format** — designed around Claude's context window handling, phase reporting style, and tool use patterns.
4. **bd comment protocol** — 'Phase: Complete' convention tested only with Claude. GPT posted it twice (duplicate), suggesting it processes the protocol differently.

## Experiment design

**Task:** Re-run doctor.go extraction (same task GPT failed at, already reverted so source is clean)

**Control:** Original spawn prompt + skill template (what GPT already failed with) — score: 2.9/5

**Variant A — Explicit steps:** Same prompt but add explicit checklist:
- STEP 1: Read doctor.go, identify function groups
- STEP 2: Create new files with extracted functions
- STEP 3: CRITICAL — Remove ALL extracted functions from doctor.go
- STEP 4: Run 'go build ./cmd/orch/' and fix any compilation errors
- STEP 5: Run tests
- STEP 6: Verify doctor.go no longer contains any functions that exist in doctor_*.go

**Variant B — Model-agnostic rewrite:** Rewrite SPAWN_CONTEXT.md to be model-neutral:
- Remove Claude-specific assumptions (narrative style, progressive disclosure)
- Add explicit verification steps after each phase
- Use numbered checklists instead of prose instructions
- Add 'VERIFY: run go build' after every file creation

## Success criteria

- If Variant A or B scores ≥3.5/5 → prompt bias confirmed, adapt prompts
- If both score <3.0/5 → model limitation, route code tasks to Claude
- Compare token usage across variants

## Dependencies

- doctor.go is back to original 2,368 lines (GPT extraction reverted)
- Baseline data from og-feat-extract-doctor-go-06feb-a099

## Deliverable

Investigation with side-by-side comparison: original prompt vs explicit vs model-agnostic. Recommendation for whether to adapt prompts or route by model.

title: orch status takes 23s - parallelize all 5 bottlenecks
description:
## Profiling Data (ORCH_STATUS_DEBUG=1 with 43 OpenCode sessions)

ListSessions: 5.8s - fetches all 43 sessions
Parallel enrichment (2 sessions): 4.1s - GetMessages per session  
GetCommentsBatch: 5.2s - beads RPC per issue
GetIssuesBatch: 4.0s - beads RPC per issue
Token fetch + risk: 4.3s - per-session token counting
Total: 23.6s

## Fix Strategy (all 5 must run concurrently)

1. ListSessions - needs shorter timeout or early return
2. Enrichment - DONE by prior agent in working tree
3. GetCommentsBatch + GetIssuesBatch - run in parallel with enrichment
4. Token fetch - run in parallel with beads batch ops
5. Account usage - DONE: already async goroutine

Key: Operations 1-4 are sequential. Making concurrent should bring total to max(individual) ~5-6s.

## Working Tree State
- pkg/opencode/client.go has GetSessionEnrichment method (KEEP)
- cmd/orch/status_cmd.go has parallel enrichment + async accounts + debug timing (KEEP)
- Target: <3s compact, <5s --all

title: Agent state read/write audit - map every field orch reads/writes across all commands
description:
## Context

orch status hit a 23.6s performance cliff (P0 incident, 2026-02-06) because it performs a distributed JOIN across 6 systems at query time. The architectural fix is a write-time materialized view (single source of truth), but we first need to know exactly what state orch reads and writes.

## Task

Trace every field of agent state that orch reads or writes, across ALL commands: status, serve, daemon, complete, spawn, abandon, frontier, review, clean, wait, resume, question, tail, send.

For each field, document:
1. What system it comes from (OpenCode API, beads CLI/RPC, tmux, disk/workspace files, registry)
2. What writes it (which command or external system)
3. How fresh it needs to be (real-time, seconds, minutes, stale-ok)
4. Whether it's derived or authoritative
5. Which orch commands consume it

## Deliverable

A complete field-level map — the requirements doc for a single-source SQLite schema. Format as a table or structured document that can be directly translated into a schema.

## Key files to examine
- cmd/orch/status_cmd.go, status_agents.go, status_health.go, status_display.go
- cmd/orch/serve_agents.go, serve_agents_status.go, serve_agents_cache.go
- cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go, complete_verify.go, complete_helpers.go
- cmd/orch/daemon.go, work_cmd.go
- cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go, spawn_helpers.go
- cmd/orch/frontier.go, review.go, wait.go, resume.go
- pkg/opencode/client.go (all API calls)
- pkg/beads/client.go, cli_client.go (all beads queries)
- pkg/tmux/tmux.go (all tmux queries)
- pkg/registry/registry.go
- pkg/verify/check.go

## Prior art
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-opencode-session-list-performance-cliff.md (today's incident)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-04-inv-agents-own-declaration-via-bd.md (lifecycle separation)
- .kb/guides/agent-lifecycle.md (four-layer state model)
- .kb/guides/status.md (status command architecture)
- .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model.md
- Constraint: "Registry is caching layer, not source of truth"
- Constraint: "orch-go agent state exists in four layers"

title: OpenCode fork audit - map session lifecycle hooks and integration points for single-source agent state
description:
## Context

We're designing a single source of truth for agent state to replace the current four-layer model (OpenCode memory, OpenCode disk, beads, tmux). Since we own forks of both OpenCode and beads, we can modify them. This investigation examines what OpenCode's fork would need.

## Task

Examine the OpenCode fork's session lifecycle code. Answer these questions:

1. **Session lifecycle**: Where are sessions created, updated (status changes, messages), persisted to disk, and deleted? Map the complete write path.

2. **Event emission**: Does OpenCode currently emit any lifecycle events (beyond SSE)? What hooks exist? Where would we add event emission for orch to subscribe to?

3. **Storage format**: What's the on-disk session format? Could OpenCode write to a shared SQLite instead of/in addition to JSON files? What would break?

4. **Three integration options** - assess each with concrete code paths and LOE:
   a. OpenCode writes to orch's SQLite directly (plugin? hook? middleware?)
   b. OpenCode emits events that orch subscribes to and materializes
   c. Orch bypasses OpenCode's storage entirely and owns session state

5. **What OpenCode provides that orch genuinely needs at runtime** vs. what's only needed for orch's own bookkeeping. Can these be separated?

## Deliverable

Concrete integration options with trade-offs. Not "we could do X" — actual code paths in the fork where hooks would go, with LOE estimates and risk assessment.

## Key files to examine (OpenCode fork at ~/Documents/personal/opencode)
- packages/opencode/src/session/index.ts (Session.list, Session.create, Session.update)
- packages/opencode/src/storage/ (Storage.read, Storage.write, Storage.list)
- packages/opencode/src/agent/ (Agent lifecycle)
- packages/opencode/src/server/ (API routes, SSE emission)
- packages/opencode/src/tool/task.ts (task tool, parentID)

## Prior art
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-opencode-session-list-performance-cliff.md (today's P0 - sequential Storage.read)
- .kb/investigations/2026-01-28-inv-investigate-opencode-native-agent-spawn.md (native agent model analysis)
- .kb/guides/opencode.md (current integration guide)
- Constraint: "OpenCode Session.list() does sequential Storage.read() per session"
- Constraint: "orch-go uses pragmatic bolt-on approach with ORCH_WORKER=1 environment variable"

Recommends Option B (SSE materialization) — zero OpenCode fork changes, extends existing monitor, orch owns its SQLite. Tests: N/A — investigation only, no code changes. No discovered work.

title: Design single-source agent state schema + migration path (depends on state audit + OpenCode audit)
description:
## Context

Two investigations completed (2026-02-06). This architect session designs the single-source agent state system based on their findings.

### Investigation 1 Output: Agent State Field-Level Audit (orch-go-21362)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-agent-state-field-level-audit.md (40KB)
- 70% of agent state is immutable after spawn (15 identity fields set once by orch spawn)
- Only 3 fields need real-time freshness: is_processing, window_exists, session_updated_at
- Proposed SQLite schema: ~25 stored columns, 4 indexes
- Registry is redundant — every field is a subset of the proposed schema
- Beads comments are the most expensive read (~700ms per issue, O(n) in agent count)
- AGENT_MANIFEST.json already has most immutable fields

### Investigation 2 Output: OpenCode Fork Audit (orch-go-21363)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-opencode-fork-audit-session-lifecycle-integration.md (22KB)
- OpenCode has 15 Bus events and 6 plugin hooks — all feed into SSE stream
- Session status (busy/idle) is IN-MEMORY ONLY — not persisted to disk
- Storage is a clean 5-method abstraction (read/write/update/remove/list) — replaceable
- Both investigations recommend Option B: SSE materialization (zero OpenCode fork changes)
- LOE estimates: Option A (plugin) 3-5 days, Option B (SSE) 3-4 days, Option C2 (shared SQLite) 1-2 weeks

### Synthesis Conclusion
Option B wins: orch subscribes to OpenCode SSE, materializes to its own SQLite. Zero fork changes. Extends existing monitor. Incremental.

## Decisions This Architect Session Must Make

1. **Phase reporting: orch-direct or beads-passthrough?**
   - Option A: Agent writes `orch phase <id> Implementing` directly to SQLite. Beads never sees phases.
   - Option B: Agent still uses `bd comment "Phase: X"`, beads emits event, orch materializes.
   - Option A is cleaner (eliminates 700ms/issue beads RPC from hot path). Option B is more conservative.

2. **Backfill strategy on SSE reconnect**
   - Full scan of GET /session API?
   - Timestamp-based (last_event_time)?
   - Hybrid (full scan on first connect, timestamp on reconnect)?

3. **Token tracking: event-driven or periodic poll?**
   - message.part.updated fires on every streaming chunk (very high frequency)
   - Could batch/debounce token updates (e.g., update every 5s during active processing)
   - Or poll GET /session/{id}/message on status queries only

4. **Migration path: incremental or cutover?**
   - Can orch status read from SQLite with fallback to old path during transition?
   - What's the verification strategy to confirm DB matches old multi-source query?

5. **DB location: global (~/.orch/state.db) or per-project (.orch/state.db)?**
   - Global = single query for cross-project status
   - Per-project = simpler but requires JOIN across DBs for orch status

6. **What happens to beads' role?**
   - Stays for issue tracking (create, close, dependencies, labels)
   - Loses agent lifecycle concern (phase tracking moves to orch)
   - Or keeps phase as secondary record (beads comments still written for audit trail)?

## Deliverable

Decision record with:
- Schema design (final, incorporating both investigation outputs)
- Write-path diagram (who writes what when)
- Migration plan with phases
- Concrete implementation issues that can be spawned from the plan

## Prior Art (READ THESE)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-agent-state-field-level-audit.md
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-opencode-fork-audit-session-lifecycle-integration.md
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-opencode-session-list-performance-cliff.md (P0 incident)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-04-inv-agents-own-declaration-via-bd.md (lifecycle separation - three orthogonal dimensions)
- .kb/guides/agent-lifecycle.md
- .kb/guides/status.md
- .kb/guides/completion.md (four-layer cleanup)

title: Beads missing 'investigation' issue type causes daemon to infer wrong skill (feature-impl instead of investigation)
description:
## Problem

When creating investigation-type work as beads issues, there's no `investigation` issue type available. The valid types are: bug, feature, task, epic, chore, merge-request, molecule, gate, agent, role, convoy, question.

Using `--type task` causes the daemon's skill inference (`pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go`) to map task → feature-impl instead of task → investigation. This means investigation work gets spawned with the wrong skill and workspace names show `feat-` prefix instead of `inv-`.

## Evidence

Today's incident: created two investigation issues (`orch-go-21362`, `orch-go-21363`) as `--type task` because `--type investigation` is invalid. Daemon spawned them as `og-feat-agent-state-read-06feb` and `og-feat-opencode-fork-audit-06feb` with feature-impl skill.

## Options

1. **Add `investigation` type to beads** — bd already has `question` type, `investigation` is a natural addition. Daemon skill inference already maps investigation → investigation skill.

2. **Use `question` type as workaround** — daemon maps question → investigation, but semantically wrong (investigations aren't questions).

3. **Use labels for skill override** — e.g., `skill:investigation` label that daemon checks before type-based inference.

Option 1 is the cleanest fix since the daemon already handles the mapping.

title: kb reflect --type open shows template placeholders as open items (7 of 22 are noise)
description:
## Problem

kb reflect --type open is meant to surface investigations with unacted recommendations. But ~7 of 22 results are template placeholders where the agent never filled in the Next: field. This is noise that drowns out the real signal (genuinely stale recommendations).

## Fix

Filter out investigations where Next: matches the template default pattern:
- `[Recommended action - close, implement, investigate further, or escalate]`
- Any Next: field still wrapped in `[...]` brackets (template placeholder convention)

Optionally: surface these separately as "investigations with unfilled Next field" — a different problem (agent quality) than "recommendations not acted on" (orchestrator follow-through).

## Files
- kb reflect lives in the kb-cli repo (~/Documents/personal/kb-cli)
- The open detection logic parses .kb/investigations/*.md files for Next: field

title: Architect review: evaluate single-source agent state SQLite design against 7 critical questions
description:
Evaluate the single-source agent state SQLite design (.kb/investigations/2026-02-06-design-single-source-agent-state.md) against 7 critical questions raised during orchestrator review.

## Questions to Evaluate

1. **Over-architecting past a simpler fix?** — 70% of fields are immutable after spawn. Could an enriched manifest cache get 90% of the win at 10% of the complexity? Is the 5.8s Session.list() fixable upstream in OpenCode instead?

2. **Operational surface area too large?** — Design turns a stateless CLI into a stateful service (SQLite DB, SSE materializer, token polling loop, backfill mechanism). What's the failure mode story? Who operates this?

3. **Repeating the registry drift pattern?** — Registry drifted because lifecycle updates weren't reliably written. SQLite relies on SSE events (lossy during disconnects), periodic polls (only when materializer alive), and orch phase commands (agents may not run). What's different enough to prevent the same drift?

4. **Dual-write transition timeline realistic?** — 4 phases over ~8 days plus 1-2 weeks shadow-read. What's the bail-out plan if shadow-read reveals hard-to-diagnose discrepancies? What's the forcing function to complete migration vs living in dual-write?

5. **Will orch phase actually get adopted?** — Requires template changes, agents on older templates won't use it, agents without orch in PATH can't use it. Would making beads phase reporting faster (index/cache) be lower risk?

6. **Does the UX improvement justify the complexity?** — Typical case is 0.7s, not 23.6s (that's the 89-session cliff). Is the real customer orch serve/dashboard (continuous reads) rather than CLI? If so, state that explicitly — it changes design constraints.

7. **Is beads-audit vs orch-runtime separation clean?** — After this change, 'what phase is agent X in?' has two answers (SQLite for current, beads for history). If they disagree (agent wrote bd comment but never ran orch phase), which wins? orch complete still closes beads issues, daemon still queries bd ready.

## Artifacts to Read

- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-design-single-source-agent-state.md (the design)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-agent-state-field-level-audit.md (field map)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-opencode-fork-audit-session-lifecycle-integration.md (integration options)
- .kb/decisions/2026-01-12-registry-is-spawn-cache.md (registry decision)
- .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model.md (four-layer model)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-04-inv-agents-own-declaration-via-bd.md (three orthogonal dimensions)
- .kb/models/dashboard-agent-status.md (priority cascade)

## Expected Output

For each question: assess whether the design's answer is sufficient or needs revision. Produce a recommendation: proceed as designed, revise specific forks, or propose a simpler alternative.

## Prior Constraints

- Registry is caching layer not source of truth
- Session idle ≠ agent complete
- State exists in four layers (OpenCode memory, OpenCode disk, registry, tmux)

title: Make orch status read immutable fields from SQLite, fall back to API for live fields
description:
Change the status read path so orch status reads immutable agent metadata (session ID, workspace, beads ID, model, directory, skill, tier, spawn time) from the local state DB instead of querying OpenCode API + registry for every field. Only query OpenCode for genuinely mutable/live fields (busy/idle status, token counts).

This is the core performance win: most of the distributed JOIN cost comes from re-fetching data that never changes after spawn.

## Context
- Depends on: orch-go-21373 (schema audit — ensures all immutable fields are in state DB)
- Field-level audit: .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-agent-state-field-level-audit.md (70% immutable)
- Current status read path: cmd/orch/status_cmd.go (multi-source reconciliation)
- Architect recommendation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-evaluate-single-source-agent-state.md (Phase A: projection-first)

## Scope
IN: status_cmd reads from state DB for immutable fields, falls back to API if DB miss, retains API queries for live fields only
OUT: Full materializer, SSE subscription, orch phase command, dashboard changes

## Success criteria
- orch status with 10+ agents is measurably faster (before/after timing)
- Graceful degradation: if state DB is empty/missing, falls back to current multi-source path
- No behavioral change in status output (same information displayed)
- Tests pass

title: Document state DB cache contract — projection cache, not source of truth
description:
Add explicit documentation that state.db is a projection cache for fast reads, NOT a source of truth. This prevents future drift where someone promotes it to authority without building reconciliation infrastructure (the exact pattern that killed the registry).

## What to document
- state.db is a spawn-time projection cache (same role as registry, but in SQLite)
- Beads owns completion status (issue open/closed)
- OpenCode owns session liveness (busy/idle/retry)
- state.db owns nothing authoritatively — it's a read optimization
- If state.db is empty or missing, system degrades to current multi-source reads
- Promotion to authority requires: reconciliation loop, discrepancy SLO, explicit migration gates (see architect evaluation)

## Where to document
- Doc comment on state DB struct/package (code-level)
- kn constrain entry (operational knowledge)
- Reference architect evaluation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-evaluate-single-source-agent-state.md

## Context
- Registry drift precedent: .kb/decisions/2026-01-12-registry-is-spawn-cache.md
- Architect evaluation recommended explicit cache contract before any authority claims

## Scope
IN: Code comments, kn entry, brief doc
OUT: Actual reconciliation implementation, migration planning

title: Phased work with evaluation checkpoints should be first-class in beads
description:
Systemic observation from the state DB projection-first architecture planning (orch-go-21376).

THE TENSION:
- Dont create future-phase issues -> amnesia kills them, work drifts
- Create all issues now -> daemon auto-spawns them without evaluating whether prior results justify proceeding
- Create with triage:review -> better, but evaluation criteria arent captured, next orchestrator doesnt know what to check

CURRENT WORKAROUND:
Gate issues (--type gate) with triage:review labels between phases. Gate descriptions capture evaluation criteria and decision outcomes (PROCEED/STOP/REVISE). Works but is manual - nothing enforces the pattern.

WHAT FIRST-CLASS SUPPORT MIGHT LOOK LIKE:
- Phase grouping within epics with gates between
- Automatic blocking: Phase B issues cant become triage:ready until gate closes with PROCEED
- Gate templates with standard evaluation structure
- Daemon awareness: daemon skips issues whose phase gate hasnt passed
- bd plan/phases command to visualize phase structure

WHY IT MATTERS:
Any non-trivial architecture work has this shape: do work, evaluate, decide whether to continue. Without tool support, pattern relies on discipline. Amnesia makes discipline unreliable.

EXAMPLE: orch-go-21376 epic (manual implementation of this pattern)

RESOLUTION: Framing question - how should beads model phased work?

title: GATE: Evaluate Phase A results before proceeding to Phase B
description:
DO NOT auto-spawn. Human evaluation checkpoint.

WHEN: After orch-go-21373, orch-go-21374, and orch-go-21375 are all complete.

EVALUATE:
1. Performance - orch status with 10+ agents, measure before/after
2. Correctness - Kill state DB, does status degrade gracefully?
3. Cache accuracy - After a day of use, compare state DB vs live API
4. Is Phase B needed? - Does Phase A satisfy dashboard/daemon?
5. Has the problem changed? - Any upstream OpenCode improvements?

OUTCOMES:
- PROCEED: Phase A works but dashboard/daemon still need live data
- STOP: Phase A is sufficient
- REVISE: Phase A revealed issues

References: .kb/investigations/2026-02-06-inv-evaluate-single-source-agent-state.md

title: orch complete hangs on Phase: Complete agents — completion ceremony unreliable
description:
## Problem
orch complete hangs indefinitely (>2min timeout) on agents that are clearly Phase: Complete with commits landed and beads comments showing full lifecycle.

## Evidence (Feb 6, 2026)
- orch-go-ev4bt: Phase: Complete, 4 beads comments showing full Planning→Implementing→Complete lifecycle, commits pushed. orch complete --batch hung >2min.
- orch-go-21368: Phase: Complete (phantom — tmux window gone, OpenCode session dead). orch complete --batch hung >2min.
- Workaround: bd close directly, bypassing all verification gates.

## Impact
Every time orch complete hangs, orchestrators bypass it with bd close, which skips verification. This erodes the integrity guarantee of the completion pipeline.

## Likely Causes
- Phantom agent: trying to query dead OpenCode session (no timeout?)
- Running agent: session alive but completion ceremony blocks on something
- bd subprocess calls during completion may trigger concurrency stampede (see ed80944f)

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Investigating orch complete hang on Phase: Complete agents. Will trace code for missing timeouts and subprocess blocking.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Implementing - Root cause: all bd CLI fallback and build subprocess calls use exec.Command with no timeout, causing indefinite hangs. Fix: convert to exec.CommandContext with appropriate timeouts.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Root cause: all bd CLI fallback and build subprocess calls used exec.Command with no timeout, causing indefinite hangs when beads daemon or bd CLI was unresponsive. Fix: converted 18 subprocess calls to exec.CommandContext with timeouts (30s bd CLI, 60s build, 120s make install). Tests: go test ./pkg/beads/... ./pkg/verify/... ./pkg/state/... - all 3 packages passed, 0 failures. No discovered work.

title: orch complete still hangs after subprocess timeout fix — root cause is deeper
description:
## Context
orch-go-21393 added CommandContext timeouts to all 18 subprocess calls in the complete path. Despite this, orch complete still hangs indefinitely.

## What was tried
- 23828fae: Added 30s/60s/120s timeouts to all exec.Command calls
- Tested on orch-go-21387 and orch-go-21393 — both hung despite fix being installed

## Likely remaining causes
1. The hang may be in the OpenCode API call path (HTTP client with no timeout?), not subprocess calls
2. Could be in the verification gate loop itself — waiting for a condition that never resolves
3. orch serve was spawning hundreds of bd processes (stampede from dashboard polling) — may need separate fix for the serve path

## Workaround
bd close <id> --reason 'summary' — bypasses all verification gates

## Impact
Every orch complete invocation hangs, forcing orchestrators to bypass with bd close. This eliminates all verification guarantees.

title: [orch-go] codebase-audit: Architecture audit of orch-go codebase focusing...
Build SSE materializer to keep state.db fresh from OpenCode session events
description:
## Goal
Build an SSE materializer goroutine that runs within orch serve. It subscribes to OpenCode's SSE event stream and materializes agent state changes into state.db in real-time. This makes state.db the authoritative read source for agent state.

## Design
- Subscribe to OpenCode SSE stream
- Listen for session events and materialize state into state.db
- Run as background goroutine started by orch serve
- Graceful reconnect on disconnect with exponential backoff

## Success Criteria
- state.db stays fresh within 2s of agent state changes
- SSE connection reconnects automatically on disconnect
- serve endpoints can read from state.db for live fields

## Context
- State.db added in 464cd2c4, status reads in 9d7698be
- orch monitor already uses SSE for monitoring
- Constraint: SSE busy->idle cannot detect true agent completion

comments.author: dylanconlin

title: Extract beads.Do() helper — eliminate 60+ callsites of beads client boilerplate
description:
## Problem
The pattern beads.FindSocketPath() -> beads.NewClient() -> client.Connect() -> defer client.Close() -> fallback to CLI is repeated 60+ times across 25+ files. pkg/verify/beads_api.go alone has 9 instances.

## Solution
Create beads.Do(projectDir, func(client *Client) error) error that centralizes socket discovery, client creation, connect/close, and CLI fallback. Eliminates ~360 lines of duplicated code.

## Acceptance Criteria
- beads.Do() helper exists in pkg/beads/
- All 60+ callsites converted (or documented exceptions)
- beads client init appears in <=5 locations
- go test ./... passes
- No behavioral changes

## Worst offenders by callsite count
- pkg/verify/beads_api.go: 9 callsites
- pkg/daemon/issue_adapter.go: 5 callsites
- pkg/daemon/dead_session_detection.go: 4 callsites
- cmd/orch/serve_beads.go: 4 callsites
- cmd/orch/handoff.go: 3 callsites

## Notes
Watch out for: subtle differences in reconnect counts and options across callsites. The defer client.Close() inside if err == nil blocks may have scoping issues during extraction.

Source: Architecture audit 2026-02-06 (two independent audits confirmed)

title: Extract verify/git_helper.go — consolidate 39 git command instances across 6 files
description:
## Problem
Identical exec.Command(git, ...) + fallback patterns copy-pasted 39 times across 6 files in pkg/verify/ and cmd/orch/complete_helpers.go. Each copy has slightly different error handling — missing a bug fix in one leaves others broken.

## Files affected
- pkg/verify/test_evidence.go: 10 instances
- pkg/verify/visual.go: 15 instances
- pkg/verify/behavioral.go: 4 instances
- pkg/verify/build_verification.go: 2 instances
- pkg/verify/escalation.go: 2 instances
- cmd/orch/complete_helpers.go: 6 instances

## Solution
Create verify/git_helper.go with shared functions:
- getChangedFiles(projectDir, since string) ([]string, error)
- getCommitHashes(projectDir, path, since string) ([]string, error)
- getFileChangesForCommit(projectDir, hash string) ([]string, error)
- Plus the relWorkspace path resolution + git log --since pattern (duplicated 4x)

## Acceptance Criteria
- verify/git_helper.go exists with shared functions
- All 39 instances converted to use helpers
- go test ./... passes
- No behavioral changes

## Notes
Watch out for: git diff fallback pattern (HEAD~5..HEAD -> HEAD~1..HEAD) varies across copies. Ensure helper preserves fallback behavior.

Source: Architecture audit 2026-02-06 (MD5 sliding window + manual verification)

title: Decompose handleAgents (756 lines) in serve_agents.go into pipeline phases
description:
## Problem
handleAgents in cmd/orch/serve_agents.go is 756 lines — the largest function in the codebase. Single HTTP handler that fetches sessions, builds workspace cache, batch-fetches beads, enriches with phase/status/gap analysis, deduplicates, filters by time/project/beads, builds investigation path cache, and serializes. 3 hard-coded dependencies (opencode.NewClient, os.Getwd, os.ReadFile). Any bug in filtering logic silently hides agents from dashboard.

## Solution
Extract into pipeline phases: buildAgentList, enrichWithBeads, filterAndDedupe, serializeResponse. Each phase testable in isolation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- handleAgents <100 lines (pipeline orchestration)
- Each phase has unit tests
- Dashboard shows same agents before/after
- go test ./... passes

Source: Architecture audit 2026-02-06

title: Decompose runSpawnWithSkillInternal (653 lines) — extract backend dispatch
description:
## Problem
runSpawnWithSkillInternal in cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go is 653 lines. Orchestrates triage bypass, concurrency limit, usage check, model resolution, skill loading, beads issue creation, workspace creation, context generation, decision gate, backend routing (4 backends: inline/headless/tmux/claude/docker), state DB recording, and event logging. A failure in any step can produce a partially-initialized agent. 22 commits of churn (highest in codebase).

## Solution
Extract backend dispatch to spawn_backends.go. Decompose into phases: validate, prepare, dispatch, record.

## Acceptance Criteria
- runSpawnWithSkillInternal <150 lines
- Backend dispatch in separate file
- go test ./... passes
- No behavioral changes

Source: Architecture audit 2026-02-06

title: Resolve beads.DefaultDir global mutation — 6 places across 5 files
description:
## Problem
beads.DefaultDir is a package-level global mutated in 6 places across 5 files:
- cmd/orch/rework_cmd.go:90
- cmd/orch/abandon_cmd.go:86
- cmd/orch/serve.go:210
- cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go:269
- cmd/orch/complete_pipeline.go:132
- cmd/orch/shared.go:428

Creates invisible coupling between command handlers. One concurrency change away from being critical.

## Solution
Route all mutations through single resolveProjectDir() function that returns (projectDir string, cleanup func(), err error) where cleanup restores beads.DefaultDir. Or pass projectDir explicitly instead of mutating global.

## Acceptance Criteria
- beads.DefaultDir mutations go through single function with cleanup
- OR beads.DefaultDir eliminated in favor of explicit parameter passing
- go test ./... passes
- No behavioral changes

Source: Architecture audit 2026-02-06

title: orch complete leaves orphan bun processes — ghost process accumulation at OS level
description:
## Problem
orch complete deletes the OpenCode session via API but does not reliably kill the underlying bun process. Over days of operation, orphan bun processes accumulate — found 52 bun processes when only 5 should be active.

## Root Cause
process.Terminate(pid) in complete_cmd.go relies on .process_id files in the workspace. If:
- Workspace was already archived before process kill
- .process_id file is missing or stale
- Agent was abandoned (not completed)
- bd close workaround was used (bypassing process cleanup entirely)
...the bun process survives indefinitely.

## Evidence (Feb 6, 2026)
- 52 bun processes running, only 5 active
- Orphans dated back to Tuesday (3+ days)
- Included: completed agent ev4bt (closed hours prior), 3-day-old investigation agent, 17 stale OpenCode sessions for orch-go
- Manual pkill required to clean up

## Impact
Memory accumulation (~100-300MB per orphan bun process). 21 orphans × ~200MB = ~4GB wasted. System degradation over multi-day operation.

## Fix Direction
1. orch complete: kill process BEFORE archiving workspace (process_id file still accessible)
2. orch abandon: ensure process cleanup runs (currently may not)
3. bd close workaround path: add process cleanup
4. Consider: periodic reaper in daemon that kills bun processes not matching active sessions
5. Consider: orch clean --processes to manually reap orphans

comments.author: dylanconlin

comments.author: dylanconlin

Created pkg/process/orphans.go with FindAgentProcesses() and FindOrphanProcesses(), integrated into clean_cmd.go with --processes flag and --all. Created follow-up: orch-go-21407 for daemon reaper integration.

title: Add go build verification gate to orch complete
description:
Problem: Agents make partial refactorings without running 'go build', leaving broken code. orch complete doesn't verify build success. Next session inherits broken state.

Solution: Add build verification to completion gates:
1. Before marking agent complete, run 'go build ./...' in workspace
2. If build fails, block completion with clear error
3. Agent must fix before completing

Evidence: Session 2026-02-06 found 23 files with incomplete Server struct refactoring, undefined 's' variable errors. Build was broken for entire session until manual revert.

Also found: 700+ .beads/issues.jsonl.tmp.* files leaked - separate issue for beads cleanup.

comments.text: Scope: 1. Add go build verification gate to orch complete flow. Investigation: Build gate ALREADY EXISTS in pkg/verify/build_verification.go. It runs 'go test -run=^$ ./...' (compile check), has blame attribution, skip memory, and is wired into orch complete via check.go checkBuild(). However, it only runs for explicitly listed skills (feature-impl, systematic-debugging, reliability-testing). The issue is that agents with unknown/no skills bypass the gate entirely because IsSkillRequiringBuildVerification returns false for unknown skills.

title: Work Graph Completed tab: sort by recency, group unverified first, reduce badge noise
description:
Completed tab currently sorts by priority (P0→P1→P2) which is wrong for completed work — urgency no longer matters. Every item shows 'NEEDS REVIEW' badge which adds no signal when universal.

Fix:
1. Default sort: closed_at descending (most recently completed first)
2. Group by verification status: unverified items on top (review queue), verified below
3. Drop 'NEEDS REVIEW' badge — instead, only show badges that differentiate: 'VERIFIED' (green) on verified items, nothing on unverified (they're the default/actionable ones)

This makes the Completed tab a proper review queue: fresh unverified work at top, already-reviewed work below.

Files likely affected:
- web/src/lib/components/work-graph-tree/ (sort logic, badge rendering)
- Possibly API endpoint for completed issues sort order

See screenshot: all 50 items show NEEDS REVIEW badge, sorted by priority, oldest completions mixed with newest.

title: Completed view: show completion summary + artifacts inline on expand (enter key)
description:
Problem: Seeing completion details requires 3 steps: select issue → press 'i' (side panel) → click 'Completion' tab. The inline expansion (enter) only shows 'Completed: date, Status: unverified, Press v/x' — nearly empty.

Fix: When pressing enter on a completed issue, show the most useful completion data inline:

1. **Completion summary** (first line/sentence of Phase: Complete comment) — this is the TLDR of what the agent did
2. **Commits** (count + first commit subject) — proof of work
3. **Artifacts** (investigation links, clickable) — the deliverables
4. **Workspace name** — for traceability

Data already exists via /api/beads/{id}/completion endpoint (used by completion-details.svelte side panel component). Just need to fetch and render it in the inline expansion instead of (or in addition to) the side panel.

The inline expansion should become the primary way to review completed work. The side panel Completion tab becomes the deep-dive for edge cases.

Current inline expansion: just date + status + v/x hint (3 lines, mostly empty)
Target inline expansion: summary + commits count + artifact links + v/x actions (dense, useful)

Files affected:
- web/src/routes/work-graph/+page.svelte (RecentlyCompletedSection rendering)
- Possibly new component or extend existing completed-item row
- Reuse /api/beads/{id}/completion endpoint already used by completion-details.svelte

Related: orch-go-21255 (enhance L1 inspection with deliverables), orch-go-21446 (completed tab sort)

title: Fix OpenCode server memory leak: add Instance cache eviction + SSE teardown cleanup
description:
## Problem

OpenCode server grows unbounded: 1.58GB after 13.5h, 8.4GB at jetsam kill. Root cause identified in orch-go-21437 investigation.

## Root Cause

1. **Unbounded Instance cache** — `Instance.provide()` caches contexts in a `Map<string, Promise<Context>>` keyed by directory. Only removed on explicit `dispose()`. With 254+ sessions, that's 254+ cached contexts with bootstrap allocations never evicted. Location: `packages/opencode/src/project/instance.ts:15`

2. **SSE teardown gap** — Normal client disconnect logs connected/disconnected (1/1), but server-side `stream.close()` during dispose shows 40/0 mismatch. Event listeners accumulate.

## Fix Required

1. Add eviction policy to Instance cache (TTL/LRU or max-live-instances cap)
2. Refactor /event SSE cleanup so `stream.close()` and abort execute identical teardown
3. Verify with multi-hour load test comparing RSS slope before/after

## Evidence

- 120-directory isolated test: RSS +50,480KB
- Deleted git directory cache persisted until explicit dispose
- SSE close-via-dispose: connected=40, disconnected=0

## References

- Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-opencode-server-memory-leak-4gb.md
- Prior: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-actually-kills-opencode-server-process.md Finding 6
- Code: packages/opencode/src/project/instance.ts, packages/opencode/src/server/server.ts

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
DEAD SESSION: Session died without completing (no active session, no Phase: Complete)

The spawned agent session died without completing. This can happen due to:
- Agent crash or context exhaustion
- OpenCode server restart
- Manual session termination

Status reset to 'open' for respawning.

title: Tighten SPAWN_CONTEXT.md completion protocol for non-Claude models
description:
## Problem

GPT-5.3-codex and other non-Claude models sometimes fail to emit explicit Phase: Complete, leaving agents in limbo. Current completion instructions are conversational and assume Claude-specific compliance patterns.

## Fix

Rewrite SPAWN_CONTEXT.md completion section with:
1. Non-conversational terminal checklist (not prose)
2. Explicit failure wording: 'If you do not emit Phase: Complete, your work will be lost'
3. Remove assumptions about Claude-specific behavior

## Evidence

Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-design-orch-handles-model-specific.md
Phase A of 3-phase layered completion reliability approach.

## References

Related: orch-go-21457 (model-specific completion)

title: Add phase-aware idle completion nudge to daemon recovery
description:
## Problem

When agents go idle without emitting Phase: Complete, the daemon's generic recovery prompt doesn't account for what phase the agent is in. An agent in Testing with test evidence should get a different nudge than one in Planning.

## Fix

Convert daemon's generic recovery prompt into a completion-aware nudge:
- If last phase is Testing/Validation/Implementing-with-test-evidence → nudge toward completion
- If last phase is Planning/early → nudge toward progress
- Use beads comments to determine current phase

## Evidence

Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-design-orch-handles-model-specific.md
Phase B of 3-phase layered completion reliability approach. Depends on Phase A (template tightening).

## References

Daemon recovery logic in pkg/daemon/

title: Introduce model behavior profiles for spawn (strict-complete vs needs-nudge)
description:
## Problem

Different models have different completion behaviors. Claude reliably emits Phase: Complete. GPT-5.3-codex sometimes doesn't. Hardcoding per-model workarounds creates maintenance burden.

## Fix

Introduce behavior profiles (e.g. strict-complete, needs-nudge) with config-driven model mapping:
- Profile defines: completion reliability, nudge strategy, timeout expectations
- Spawn reads profile to adjust template language and daemon recovery behavior
- Avoids per-model template duplication

## Evidence

Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-design-orch-handles-model-specific.md
Phase D (hardening) of layered completion reliability approach. Depends on Phase A + B.

## References

Config: ~/.orch/config.yaml model routing
Spawn: cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go model selection

title: Remove Redis lock from price-watch scraper (after pw-8928 validation)
description:
## Problem

Redis distributed lock in ScrapeCliJob causes 3 of 7 incident root causes in price-watch collection system. Capsule already enforces concurrency=1, making the lock redundant.

## Fix

1. Run production collection with lock disabled behind feature flag
2. Confirm no concurrent execution detected
3. Remove lock code entirely

## Evidence

Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-design-scraper-reliability-strategy-price.md
Phase 1 of 3-phase scraper reliability hardening.

## Note

This is price-watch domain work. Issues created here for tracking until price-watch repo is available.

title: Unify price-watch self-healing into single CollectionRunHealthCheckJob
description:
## Problem

Multiple overlapping self-healing jobs in price-watch create confusion and race conditions. Orphaned quotes, stale ScrapeJobs, and run completion readiness are checked by different jobs with different cadences.

## Fix

Merge into single CollectionRunHealthCheckJob running every 5 min that:
- Finds all active runs (in_progress + incomplete)
- For each: detects orphaned quotes, stale ScrapeJobs (>15 min), run completion readiness
- Single dispatch path with dedup (check if quote already has pending/processing job)
- Clear logging: 'Run #47: 3 orphaned quotes redispatched, 1 stale job superseded, 95% complete'

## Evidence

Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-design-scraper-reliability-strategy-price.md
Phase 1 of 3-phase scraper reliability hardening. Closes pw-8933 (job overlap question).

## Note

Price-watch domain work. Tracked here until price-watch repo is available.
close_reason: Price-watch domain work, re-file in price-watch repo when beads is set up there. See .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-design-scraper-reliability-strategy-price.md for full context.

title: Add run-level alerting and canary gate to price-watch collection
description:
## Problem

No observability into collection run health. Failures are discovered manually. No pre-collection validation catches browser/proxy/login issues before committing to a 684-quote run.

## Fix

1. Run lifecycle Slack notifications: run starts, completes (with success rate), stuck (no progress in 30 min), failed
2. Canary-before-collection gate: auto-run quick_test.yaml (6 quotes) before full collection. If canary fails, block and alert.
3. Queue/persona status API endpoint for dashboard

## Evidence

Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-design-scraper-reliability-strategy-price.md
Phase 2 of 3-phase scraper reliability hardening.

## Note

Price-watch domain work. Tracked here until price-watch repo is available.

title: bd sync killed by JSONL import with 1788+ issues — beads changes can't sync
description:
## Problem

bd sync fails with 'signal: killed' when importing JSONL in orch-go repo (1788 issues). The import step runs before sync and gets OOM-killed or times out. This means beads changes are NOT syncing — we're working around it by committing .beads/issues.jsonl directly via git.

## Impact

- Beads data could drift between clones
- Git hooks that depend on bd sync may silently fail
- bd doctor shows 'fresh clone' warnings because no database exists

## Reproduction

```
$ bd sync
→ JSONL content differs from last sync
→ Importing JSONL first to prevent stale DB from overwriting changes...
Error: importing (hash mismatch): import failed: signal: killed
```

Also fails with --no-db flag.

## Likely cause

JSONL import trying to hydrate all 1788 issues into memory or SQLite at once. Needs streaming/chunked import or skip-import option for sync.

## Workaround

Direct git operations: git add .beads/issues.jsonl && git commit && git push
close_reason: Phase: Complete - Added scripts/bd-sync-safe.sh and updated AGENTS.md to use SQLite direct-mode sync with automatic DB bootstrap; verified bd sync no longer reproduces signal-killed import path.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Reproduction verified: Before fix, 'bd sync' failed with 'importing (hash mismatch): import failed: signal: killed'. After switching this repo to SQLite direct mode (config + safe sync wrapper), verification passed with: 'bd sync --no-pull --no-push' (Sync complete), 'bd sync --import-only' (Import complete), and './scripts/bd-sync-safe.sh --no-pull --no-push' (Sync complete).

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Added scripts/bd-sync-safe.sh and updated AGENTS.md to use SQLite direct-mode sync with automatic DB bootstrap; verified bd sync no longer reproduces signal-killed import path.

title: orch status takes 30s+ and times out — primary monitoring command unusable
description:
## Problem

orch status hangs for 30+ seconds and times out. This is the primary command for monitoring spawned agents. Without it, orchestrators fall back to tmux list-windows which only shows window names, not phase/progress/beads data.

## Impact

- Can't monitor agent progress without manual tmux inspection
- Orchestrator flies blind during multi-agent sessions
- Previously filed as orch-go-21361 (P0, closed) — either regressed or fix was incomplete

## Reproduction

```
$ orch status
# hangs >30 seconds, no output
```

## Prior fix

orch-go-21361 'orch status takes 23s - parallelize all 5 bottlenecks' was closed as fixed. Either:
1. The fix regressed
2. The fix only addressed some bottlenecks
3. A new bottleneck was introduced (e.g. OpenCode server under load with multiple agents)

## Workaround

tmux list-windows -t workers-orch-go | grep pattern

title: bd create silently loses issues on JSONL hash mismatch
description: When bd create encounters a JSONL hash mismatch, it prints a warning but the issue never makes it into issues.jsonl. The user sees a success message with an issue ID (e.g., orch-go-c7vj2) but grep confirms the ID doesn't exist in the file. Downstream: orch spawn --issue fails with 'not found', wasting time debugging. Observed Feb 7 2026 — two bd create calls both showed success + hash mismatch warning, neither issue persisted. Workaround: use bd --sandbox create. Fix: bd create should either (1) fail hard on hash mismatch instead of printing a warning, or (2) ensure the write succeeds regardless of hash state.

title: orch abandon resets crash-free streak — routine cleanup shouldn't count as recovery
description: When orch abandon is used to clean up stale/dead agents, the crash-free streak resets to 0 because agent_abandoned is treated as a recovery intervention. This is a false positive — abandoning stale agents is routine hygiene, not crash recovery. Streak went from 6h 32m to 9m after cleaning up 2 dead agents. The streak should only reset on actual failures: service OOM kill, zombie process accumulation requiring manual kill, service crash requiring restart, corrupted database requiring rebuild. Fix: either (1) don't count agent_abandoned as a recovery event, or (2) distinguish 'voluntary abandon' from 'forced abandon due to infrastructure failure'.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Scope: 1. Fix crash-free streak false positive where agent_abandoned resets streak 2. Distinguish infrastructure failures (OOM, crash, zombie, DB corruption) from hygiene operations (orch abandon) 3. Preserve streak concept, filter reset events 4. Reference: serve_operator_health.go, observation/intervention decision, Phase 3 design

These are intentionally PPID=1 — they're managed by launchd, not orphaned from orch. Fix: whitelist known legitimate PPID=1 processes (overmind, tmux, system paths) or only flag processes whose command matches orch-related binaries (bun, opencode, node, orch).

title: [orch-go] systematic-debugging: HTTP client connection pool in orch serve accum...
Fix unbounded HTTP client connection pool in orch serve
description: HTTP transport accumulates persistent connections without MaxIdleConns/IdleConnTimeout. After 15min: 330 goroutines (123 inbound + 97+92 outbound readLoop/writeLoop pairs). C1 violation per unbounded resource decision. Fix: configure http.Transport on all outbound clients.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Reproduction verified: launched temporary orch serve on :4350, captured baseline pprof goroutines (total=10, persistConn readLoop=1/writeLoop=1), hammered /api/agents and /api/attention plus 100 short-lived /api/events connections, then rechecked pprof (total=11, readLoop=1/writeLoop=1). Connection goroutines stayed bounded instead of growing with polling.

Audit existing caches in the codebase, add a shared cache type if one doesn't exist, ensure all call sites specify bounds.

title: Add probe awareness to investigation skill
description: The investigation skill doesn't know about probes. When kb context injects model content into SPAWN_CONTEXT, the investigation skill should guide agents to produce a probe (30-50 lines, .kb/models/{name}/probes/) instead of a full investigation (300 lines, .kb/investigations/). Options: (1) mode flag on investigation skill (--probe), (2) auto-detect model injection and switch template, (3) separate probe skill. The spawn-time detection already exists (HasInjectedModels in config.go), and probe guidance is already injected in context_template.go — but the investigation SKILL.md itself doesn't mention probes. title: Make triage: labels mutually exclusive in bd label add
description: When adding triage:ready, auto-remove triage:review (and vice versa). Currently both labels can coexist, which causes the daemon to silently skip issues. Hit this in production: architect labeled issues triage:review, orchestrator added triage:ready without removing review, daemon never picked them up. The fix is in bd label add — detect triage: prefix labels and enforce mutual exclusion. Small change, prevents a silent failure mode.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Reproduction verified: in isolated temp repos, baseline /Users/dylanconlin/bin/bd allowed both triage:review + triage:ready on same issue; after building /tmp/bd-triage-local from updated beads cmd/bd/label.go, adding triage:ready removed triage:review and adding triage:review removed triage:ready (label list returned only the newly-added triage label).

title: work-graph: add 'Ready to Complete' section for agents at Phase: Complete but not yet closed
description: The work-graph has a blind spot: agents that reach Phase: Complete but haven't had orch complete run on them are invisible. They're not actively working (so they fade from the Issues tab), and they're not closed (so they don't appear in Completed). This is the most actionable state — these are the agents the orchestrator needs to review next — yet it has no home in the UI. Tonight the daemon processed ~16 issues but the Completed tab shows '2 days ago' as most recent because all those agents are in this liminal state. Need: (1) A 'Ready to Complete' or 'Awaiting Review' section in work-graph that shows agents at Phase: Complete with in_progress beads status. (2) This section should be prominent — it's the primary action queue for the orchestrator. (3) Include agent runtime, token usage, and completion timestamp so orchestrator can prioritize review order. Data source: the attention system already has PHASE_COMPLETE signals, and orch status shows these agents. The work-graph just doesn't surface them distinctly.

title: Untracked sessions count against concurrency limit
description: The concurrency check extracts beadsID from session title. Untracked sessions have pattern like [orch-go-untracked-123] which still matches, then fails the 'is closed' check since there's no beads issue. Fix: exclude sessions with 'untracked' in beadsID pattern.
close_reason: Phase: Complete - Tests: go test ./cmd/orch/... - all passed (3s). Fixed untracked sessions counting against concurrency limit by adding isUntrackedBeadsID filter in checkConcurrencyLimit (spawn_cmd.go:476-483). Pattern matches daemon.DefaultActiveCount.

title: Add --compact flag to orch status (default to running agents only)
description: From investigation 2026-01-16-inv-analyze-orchestration-session-hit-context: orch status outputs 437 lines for 182 agents. This wastes ~5K tokens of context. Should default to showing only running/recent agents (compact), with --all for full listing. Success: orch status output < 50 lines by default.
close_reason: Optimized orch status from 15s/437 lines to 1.7s/75 lines via compact mode (shows running + needing-attention agents) and IsSessionProcessing optimization (skip HTTP for stale sessions). Use --all for full output.

title: [orch-go] systematic-debugging: Agents being marked completed in registry immed...
Operator health dashboard: behavioral system health for non-code-readers
description:
Dylan's verification surface is currently theater - agent self-reports, agent-written tests, dashboard that was itself broken. The actual verification signal is 'the system crashed so often I couldn't ignore it.' Build a single view (route on existing dashboard or standalone) that shows behavioral health signals readable without knowing Go:

1. Crash-free session streak (days since last manual recovery: kill zombie, restart crashed service, rebuild corrupted DB, abandon infra-killed agent)
2. Resource ceiling metrics (goroutines, heap, child processes, FDs vs baseline - from orch-go-qsh3h)
3. Investigation rate (rolling 30-day count with threshold indicator at 50 = fire-fighting signal)
4. Defect class clustering (top defect classes in last 30 days - from orch-go-fri4i taxonomy)
5. Agent health ratio (completions vs abandonments, rolling 7-day)
6. Process census (current child processes, any orphans with PPID=1)

Design principle: every metric should answer 'is the system healthy or am I about to drive off a cliff' without requiring the operator to read implementation code. This is Infrastructure Over Instruction applied to human verification.

Depends on: orch-go-qsh3h (resource ceiling), orch-go-fri4i (defect taxonomy). Can start design immediately, wire up data sources as dependencies land.

Reference: .kb/investigations/2026-02-07-inv-system-reliability-crisis-diagnosis-and-fix.md

Active agent titles sometimes unhelpful (e.g. 'Orchestrator skill says')
description:
## Problem

Active agent cards show titles that aren't meaningful. Example: 'Orchestrator skill says' doesn't tell the user what the agent is actually doing.

## Evidence

Screenshot shows an active agent with title 'Orchestrator skill says' - this is the start of the task description but truncated unhelpfully.

## Root Cause

Title is derived from task description which may start with context rather than the actual task. For investigations spawned with questions, the question text becomes the title.

## Expected

Show a more useful title:
- Use beads issue title if available
- Fall back to skill + short task summary
- Consider extracting the actual task from longer descriptions

## Files

description:
Core CLI command that displays decidability state in readable format.

```
READY TO RELEASE (3)

BLOCKED - would unblock most first (2)

ACTIVE (2)

```

**Data sources:**
- Ready: `bd ready` logic

title: Trial: One week daemon-first workflow (Jan 6-13)
description:
## Purpose

Build trust in daemon through direct experience, not theory.

## Rules

- Issues via `bd create` → daemon picks them up
- No `orch spawn` for workers (unless true emergency)
- Meta-orchestrator produces issues, not spawns
- `--bypass-triage` only for orchestrator sessions or emergencies

## Observe

- Skill inference accuracy
- Context quality
- Timing (30-60s delay)
- Completion quality

## Capture

- Every friction point
- Every bypass (with reason)
- Every 'I wish I could spawn' impulse

## End Date

2026-01-13 - Review results and decide next steps
close_reason: Trial successful - daemon-first workflow adopted. Strategic Orchestrator Model decision (2026-01-07) formalized this: daemon coordinates, orchestrator comprehends.
